speaker: interviewer
text: I'm [? Esther ?] [? Ditherenfeld, ?] and I'm interviewing Mr. Aharon Golub. Mr. Golub, you were telling me before the tape ended about the Russian commissar on his horse.

speaker: interviewee
text: The German.

speaker: interviewer
text: The German. I'm sorry. The German commissar.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He used to come-- he used to surprise the people. Nobody ever knew when he would show up. And when he came, he always managed to find a victim. Either he-- he-- he humiliated people by lashing-- beating a person with a whip-- in front of all the workers. And on one occasion, as I mentioned before, he had killed a person not too far from myself. There's been another incident where he had found some objection about some-- somebody's work. And he-- he-- he took out his pistol, and he shot-- and he was about to shoot the man in his head, and the pistol did not fire. So he-- he aimed-- he aimed again. And twice, the pistol did not fire. And he said to the man, you see-- you seem to be some kind of a devil. I guess you are destined to live. And he let him go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So but this is horrified all the people. It was just a frightening scene. I know my-- my mother used to-- before my sister used to go to work with me, my mother would take some black ashes and-- and-- and-- and make her-- and dress her in such a way to look ugly so she wouldn't be noticed and-- and-- and raped by-- because-- by the commisar-- by the Russ-- by the German commissar.

speaker: interviewee
text: He used to-- when he noticed a good looking girl that he liked, he would call over the foreman and-- and just point this girl out to him and say that whatever time, like tomorrow night at such and such time, this girl is to be brought to my house. And-- and all the women lived in constant fear that they would be selected by him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my mother always was concerned about my sister. My sister was, at that time, 17 years old approximately, and she was a very beautiful girl. And my mother dressed her in a long dress and gave her a scarf to look like a babushka, you know? And she made the dark under the eyes so she would not be noticed and always told her never to look up, always like look to the ground. So we lived in terrible fear. Did that protect her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank God nothing happened to her during-- during the-- during while we lived in ghett-- in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. And your younger sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My younger sister was too little. She didn't go to work. And my mother did not go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because she was running the photography.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she was running the photography, and-- I suppose that's the main reason, I guess. Yes. In the ghetto, things were getting worse all the time. In the winter, people did not have any firewoods. And people used to gr-- go during the night and try to demolish some structures--

speaker: interviewer
text: Within the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Even in the ghetto. Anything that could be broken, people would break-- fences and things, little-- little gardens, whatever. And the place looked like a real mess. People were desperate. And people lived in constant fear. People used to come up with rumors why, perhaps, we are not going to be killed because we thought that that road is very important to the Germans for their war machine. And we always felt that as long as that road is being-- is not completed yet, we-- we are safe. But apparently the Germans had different plans. The-- something terrible used to be done to us during the night. The-- the Ukrainian police used to come and start knocking on the doors and-- and wake up all the people in the middle of the night and run and chase them all into the square and line them up in rows. And people used to come out with their nightclothes, and-- and-- no matter what condition you are at the time of the knock on the window, that's how you had to come out in the street. And sometimes the people would stay there for hours and wait and didn't know what's-- what's going to happen to them. And-- and-- and suddenly, the commissar arrived on his horse, and he delivered a speech, mostly about cleanliness, about he heard that the ghetto is dirty, and he's going to have inspections-- all kind of nonsense of that kind. And it turned out later on that this was a calculated arrangement. They were always afraid perhaps the Jews would rebel when the final solution would come. Once they realize they were being taken to die, they thought there would be perhaps some-- some-- the Jews would cause some trouble. And in order to get people used to-- to those meetings-- so every-- every couple of weeks, they would-- they would pull the people out in the middle of the night. And everybody got used to that this is one of the sufferings that we-- we have to-- we have to endure. And-- and-- but they knew that one of these days when they'll make a decision for the final liquidation of the ghetto that the Jews would already be-- be accustomed to come out to the square. And that's exactly what happened. During that time, my father wanted to spare us the humiliation of-- of being taken out during the night into the-- into the square. So we built-- we had-- in our-- in our building, there was a small basement. And my father had managed to-- to find old wood and cover the floor of the-- of the basement where the-- where the door used to be. So when you walked into-- to the-- to the the room, you couldn't-- you weren't aware that it was a basement underneath. And from the wall in the kitchen, we were able to lift up two planks of wood. And we used to be able to slide in into the basement from the kitchen. So every time when-- when they used to start knocking on the windows and chasing the entire ghetto into-- into the square, we used to slide in into the basement. And we would sit there for an hour or two, as long as it took. And we were spared the humiliation of being taken out int-- in-- into the-- into the square.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you help your father build this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I was the-- my father did it by himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: All by himself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, I was aware that they're doing it. I don't recall-- I don't think I was-- I did much to help. But he built it. And there was other people in that house that were liv-- there was also another photographer. So this man, together with my mother, ran-- ran the business. And so they were also included into this hiding place. So I-- I think that this gentleman helped my father to-- to build it. So every time when this used to happen, we used to slide in into the basement and wait out till we heard the noise of the people returning. Then we came-- we came out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your family tell you about what to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was-- you grow up very quickly in times like this. Everybody knew exactly that-- that we are living on borrowed time. We heard from time to time that liquidation was done in this town, and a liquidation was done in another town, and another ghetto was eliminated. And we were always saying that perhaps we will be spared because we are working on the construction of this important road. So people-- you can talk yourself into all kind of things to-- to try to-- to give you some hope even if it's not real hope. And then on August 26, the-- the-- there was another roundup. And we also sl-- had slid into our hiding space, and we were 13 people.

speaker: interviewee
text: 13 People. There was just barely standing room there because this was-- this-- this was not meant for us to stay longer than a few hours at a time. And people were all chased out to the square. And we waited an hour, and two hours, and three hours, and four hours. And the people did not return. And then we-- we heard screaming and yelling and-- we knew immediately that this was the final hour of our existence. And we remained hidden in this place for three days. And there was no sanitary condit-- preparations of any kind. There was no-- no food, no drinking water, just standing-- standing room only. And we definitely knew that this was the final-- the final act because number one, the people never returned back. Number two, the next morning we heard all our Christian neighbors coming into the house and-- and fighting over our possessions. And from time to time, we heard shooting and-- and screaming of other people. Apparently we were not the only ones that hiding places. So the-- the Ukrainian and the Germans were-- were going through the houses and looking for fake ceilings and extra-- looking for hiding places. And every time they discovered one, they would take the people out and shoot them right in front of their homes. So we-- we knew that this was really the final-- the final solution. But we were so petrified, we didn't know what to do. And during the day, the Germans used to-- they came and-- the Ukrainians mostly. They were-- they were-- they are the police, and they used to be supervised by some German officers. But the job was mostly done by the Ukrainian police. And they used to come into the house. And from time to time, they would hammer on the floors with-- with the butts of their rifles to see if there's hollow-- hollow space. But since our basement was in a little room outside of the house, we were never discovered. And with us were-- were also two babies that belonged to two-- to two of the women. And-- and the babies cried. They were hungry. They wanted food. And every time is so happened-- It looked like every time there was a critical moment when people came to-- to search the house or look, they started to cry. And-- and the men subsequently put pillows on top of the babies, and the babies were suffocated. And one of the mothers, I remember, became so hysterical that she had to be subdued by-- by-- by the-- by some of the men.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a big tragedy. We were sitting in that-- in this little basement. There were two dead babies. And our life wasn't-- wasn't worth anything neither. And after three nights, we just figured that we're going to die here anyway if you don't go out. So we-- we came out and we-- Did you know what was happening at the time with the babies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there. But I mean, there's nothing I could do about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk about it with your family? Did anybody--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nobody talked to anybody, because our life-- we thought we were next. And it was very painful to-- to-- to-- to-- to see the mothers crying and tearing pieces of themselves. And-- and-- and I'm sure that the people that-- the men that had to do it felt terrible themselves. But everybody felt that we are next. So during the night-- we opened the basement after the third night and it is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who made that decision?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess my father and the other men and the other people. This-- we decided we have to go out because we're going to suffocate there. So we-- we opened, and we came outside. And we-- it was dark and quiet. And we ran into the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: All of you together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not all of us, but we separated. I mean, our family was a-- a unit, and the other family-- there were like three families. And-- and-- and everybody disappeared in the dark. But we were together, all five of us. And we ran in the direction of the woods. And as we were running, we were intercepted by Ukrainians that were lying in the ditches with rifles. And we thought they are going to kill us. But apparently they were not interested in killing anybody. They were afraid to create noise. Perhaps the Germans would hear. They only wanted to rob us of everything that we had, the few possessions we had. So we had some coats and shoes, and they-- they just-- they stripped this practically to our-- to our-- they took away everything they could. If-- if we had anything-- we had any rings, any-- any clothes, and they let us run practically naked.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they leave you with? What were you wearing when they left?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, they-- they left us-- they left me with my pants and a little shirt. But they took away my sweater, and they took away my coat. And they took away my hat, and they took away my shoes. And they did the same thing for my mother and my sister and my father. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your other sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my other sister, too. We were all together. So we were ver-- very, very nervous and very frightened. And we were delighted that they didn't kill us. So we continue running into the woods. And-- and my-- we didn't know my father had remembered that one of our loyal workers-- one of the mechanics that used to run one of the factories-- lived-- was a Polish man, and he lived in a-- not too far away from us. So we went in the direction of his home. And when we came to him in the middle of the night, we knocked on his door. And he received us fairly well. He put us in a barn, and he gave us some blankets, and they gave us something to eat. And we stayed there for about two days, in-- hidden in his barn. And then he came in, the man, and he was all white and shaking from fear. And he said that-- that the-- that there's a big danger to him and his family and to us. And-- and-- and if some neighbor of his gets a wind that we are there and they inform to the police, we'll all be slaughtered. And he suggest that we should go into a area away from his home, perhaps two miles into the woods. We considered that to be sort of a secluded area in the forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went there, and we were waiting there. And his wife used to come and bring us food. The-- the-- the peasants in that area used to go run to pick berries in the woods that time of year. And they used to use a pail to collect the berries. And when it was all done, they would-- it was a tradition-- they would cover it with a white cloth-like to take it home. So she put the food in a pail. And it appear as if she-- if she was going to collect berries or something like that. And she used to bring us the food for about a week or so.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the farmer came, and he said that it's too danger-- it really was a rather small forest in relation to all the other forests in the area. And he suggested that perhaps he knows that in another forest, quite a distance from here-- from here, there are other Jews hiding. And maybe if we went into that area, perhaps we would feel safer to be with-- with-- with other people. And we realized that he really wanted us to go because he could no longer sustain her coming and bringing us food, and he was afraid for his life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we thanked him very much. And we walked during the night mostly and in the evening. And we did find the place. It was near a town called Korets. Jews that were-- ran away from that city were hiding in those forests. And we sort of settled next to them. My father had a small little knife, so we-- we cut twigs. And it was beginning to get-- it was already-- it was just after-- it was just past the high holidays. It was after Yom Kippur. And it was getting sort of chilly at night, but what else could we do? So we had a fire burning all the time. And we sat most of the day and night next to the fire. And we have a little hot, just enough to protect us from the dew in the morning. And we were there about a month. And we used to-- and we used to go around begging the farmers for food. The Polish farmers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In-- in that area were mostly Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ukrainian?

speaker: interviewee
text: We would go, and-- and-- sometimes they would be very mean to us, and they would chase the dogs after us. And here and there, some people were nice, and they would give us some potatoes. And we were able to bake the potatoes in the fire and-- and sort of stay alive. We had no clothes. We were all very dirty. We were full of lice. There was nowhere to-- to bathe or to wash. There was no sanitary conditions of any kind. But we just lived from day to day. And we just didn't know what to do with ourselves. And after a month or so being at that place, we were suddenly attacked by-- by a group of Ukrainian police. They hunted us down like animals, and they came in shooting machine guns from every side. And-- and at that-- and they-- we all ran in different directions not knowing what happened to each one of us. And when everything was over, then later on-- I had hidden in a bush, and nobody found me. And I thought I was the only survivor left from the-- from a group of about 40 people, perhaps, was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then as I was hiding, I heard some-- some lady crying, children, where are you? Where are you? And I didn't know who the woman was because her voice did not sound familiar to me. And then she started to call names. And she called my father's name. She called my sister's name and my name. So I recognized that this is my mother. So I ran. And I found her. And I looked at her, and she was shot in her arm. And her bone was shot at, and she was lying probably for hours. And she lost-- God knows-- most of her blood. She was in terrible condition. So I-- I-- I grabbed her, and I had hidden her under the bush where I was lying there. And with her underwear, I made a sling for her. [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and then I remembered that we-- at the fire where we were sitting, there was-- perhaps there were some potatoes left. And I went to the place, and the fire was still-- it burned for-- for so many days. There was a lot of coal, and there was still heat. There was still fire in the place. So I-- I put in a few potatoes in there. And I ran away because I was afraid to sit next to the fire in case if they would come again. And maybe a half an hour later or an hour later when I felt the potatoes are baked, I took a dash to the place, and I pulled out a potatoes. And I came, and I fed my mother a little bit. But she was very weak. She kept on fainting on me all the time. And somehow we-- we slept through the night in the forest, just the two of us. There was no other voice. If there was anybody that survived, we never knew about it. And we decided the-- the-- on the next day that we will return to the same forest where that mechanic, my father's worker, used to live because we just didn't know what else to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a long way to walk. And it took us a whole day to-- to-- to walk. Your mother walked?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother kept on fainting all the time. And I propped her up and-- and on the way, I got some milk for her from some farmers. And as we were coming, some farmers told us that two little girls passed through here the-- the other day. And one of them said her name was-- was Golub. The Ukranians-- they cannot pronounce the G, so they say, 'Hol,' like a H. So they said, the girl, she was-- she said she was 'Holub's' daughter. So I knew my little sister was alive. And to my big surprise, when I arrived to the location where we were going-- where-- where our friend used to live, I found my-- my little sister. And God knows how she ever managed-- a little girl-- to find a way from such a distance. So she was there. And she escaped practically naked because during the day, sometimes, we would take off our shirts and put 'em over the smoke of the fire so-- so the lice would-- would-- would fall. You know, they used to-- that's how we kept ourselves as clean as possible. So apparently she was doing that, and she just ran away with-- with a little underwear. So some farmers on the way gave her a little dress or something. And when I arrived to the place there, I found another lady that was there all the time-- she never left that place-- with two daughters. So already we were a small group. And there was another single lady. I don't know how she got there ever. She was there also. So we already were a little group of people, and it felt better not being alone. And I went to the-- to my father's friend, the mechanic. His name was Maschik. And I told them what happened to my mother. And he gave me some vodka or alcohol. And he gave me some cotton, and he told me to put compresses on her hand. And-- and believe it or not, it started to heal. I mean, the bone was shattered, of course. But, I mean, the wound itself got to look pinker and sort of a little bit better. And every day, I changed her bandage with the alcohol. And she cried so much because the pain. It burned her terribly, but she knew that we had to do it. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So then the woman that we found there-- she was a well-to-do woman. She used to have a very large store of-- she used to sell material for-- for-- for dresses. In our town, nothing was ready-made. You know, you used to buy the material and go to a tailor and or a-- a seamstress, and they would make you the dress. So she was a well-off woman. She told me that she had managed to salvage some gold. And she had it sew-- sewed in in her son's jacket. Oh, I'm sorry. She had two daughters and a son, a little boy. And the son had a little quilted jacket-like. And in the jacket, she said, she had the gold sewn in. And she was going to go and negotiate with some farmer and give him the money, perhaps.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a very capable woman. She was a businesswoman. Her husband was dead a long time ago. And she said she-- she had spoken to someone, and she thinks that with-- for the money she has, perhaps they will make us a hiding place and-- and keep all of us there. So here again, we had some hope of perhaps surviving. And then suddenly, the Ukrainians attacked us again. Out of nowhere, they appeared with-- with-- with semi machine guns, firing from-- from every side. And-- and-- and before I turned around, I-- I saw this big policeman standing with this rifle, aiming it right at me. And I figured, here, I'm a goner. And my mother was lying in a little-- I made a little hut for her, you know, from twigs. So she heard the commotion. So she-- she sort of crawled out, and when she saw-- when she saw that he was about to shoot me, she gave a tremendous yell. And he turned around, and he shot her-- [SOBBING]-- shot her ins-- instead of me. [CRYING] And I ran away again. And I ran like a gazelle. And they shot after me continuously, but they didn't hit me. [CRYING] And-- and I ran and ran and ran till I came to some place, to a farmer, and I asked for some food. And he told me-- he gave me food. And he told me that he knows that in a certain forest, there was some people. Some people are living-- Jewish people are hiding there. And he gave me directions-- he gave me directions how to go there. [PAUSES] And-- and I came-- found the people. And I stayed with these people for a while and-- in the woods. And it became very cold already. I didn't have any shoes, and I didn't have an clothing except some rags that I accum-- got along the way from some farmers. And the winter was a very severe winter. And we used to-- and so-- I used to go-- we used to go around begging for food from the farmers. And most of the farmers would sort of throw you a few potatoes. And we-- we could've survived if-- if we were left alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were these people that you were living with? Can you tell me a little bit about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- I-- When I-- when I came there, I found the people which were with us in the hiding place. There was a father-- a father and a moth-- a husband and a wife. And he had a sister and two children. And the baby was one of their babies that was-- that was suffocated in-- in the hiding place. But they-- they-- they were dressed well. When they separated from us, they were not intercepted. And whatever blankets and clothes they had, they were able to keep it with them. And I-- and we dug up-- we dug a hole in the ground. And they slept all four of them-- all five of them-- together, and they kept-- body heat, I guess-- they kept themselves reasonably warm. And I laid by myself in a corner. And there were people there that were able to-- to build much more substantial huts, like a log-- almost like a log cabin. They were strong men. And they got together groups of people to build a big log cabin. And somehow they obtained one of those old-fashioned little stoves, that you could-- a wood-- a wood-firing stove. And their place was fairly warm, but they were very crowded, and they would not let me go in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aharon, I'm going to stop you now.
